# MATH-CS COMPASS

> **Bridging Pure & Applied Mathematics with Computer Science and Machine Learning**

MATH-CS COMPASS is a free educational platform that addresses the gap where computer science students struggle with mathematical foundations while mathematics students lack awareness of practical applications. The curriculum develops rigorous mathematical foundations and introduces application domains at the moment sufficient tools are in hand — creating a virtuous cycle of **math → application preview → deeper math → richer application.**

## Philosophy

Mathematics is the study of **structure**. This platform offers an accessible yet principled look at the mathematical blueprints behind modern computation. Specific tools and frameworks age quickly; the mathematical foundations beneath them do not.

The curriculum is organized as **two worlds + a bridge + application viewpoints**:

- **The Core (Section I)** — Linear Algebra to Algebraic Foundations. The common language every later section speaks.
- **The Continuous World (Section II)** — Calculus, Analysis, Functional Analysis, Smooth Manifolds. Smooth change, limits, completeness, curvature.
- **The Discrete World (Section IV)** — Logic, Algorithms, Combinatorics, Discrete Topology, Category Theory, Cryptography. Finite and countable structures.
- **The Bridge (Section III)** — Probability and Statistics. Where the counting of the discrete meets the integration of the continuous and the two turn out to be one.
- **Application Viewpoints (Section V)** — Machine Learning. Not final destinations but high vantage points, where prior tools work together on real problems and reveal deeper mathematics worth pursuing.

### Three Application Horizons

These are **not terminal goals** but recurring **viewpoints** — high vantage points where the tools built so far come together on a class of real-world problems and reveal deeper mathematics worth pursuing.

1. **Geometric Deep Learning (GDL)** — *the primary structural thread.* It develops along two legs. The **continuous leg** runs manifolds → Riemannian geometry → representation theory → equivariant networks (SO(3)/SE(3) symmetry). The **discrete leg** runs message-passing graph neural networks → simplicial complexes and the Hodge Laplacian → simplicial neural networks and topological deep learning (a subfield of GDL that generalizes pairwise interaction to higher-order). Both legs are in place, and they are designed to eventually rejoin through Hodge theory, where differential forms on manifolds and simplicial cochains express the same structure.
2. **Categorical Deep Learning (CDL)** — *active parallel track, foundation-first.* Compositionality, structural abstraction, and functorial reasoning. The category-theory foundation in Section IV is substantially built — categories, functors, and natural transformations through adjunctions, the Yoneda lemma, and limits/colimits — and keeps growing. The Section V bridge page is not yet written.
3. **Quantum Computation** — *treated as a viewpoint reached through symmetry and cryptography.* Unitary evolution, spectral decomposition, and Hilbert space geometry. It appears concretely in Section I's quantum computation page and Section IV's Shor's algorithm, which together motivate the post-quantum cryptography track.

## Author & Meta

- **Author**: Yusuke Yokota
- **Education**: B.S. Mathematics & B.S. Computer Science, Western Washington University
- **Curriculum data version**: 4.6 (Last Updated: 2026-07-19)
- **Website**: [https://math-cs-compass.com](https://math-cs-compass.com)
- **GitHub**: [https://github.com/yusukey01](https://github.com/yusukey01)

## Curriculum Outline

The curriculum spans **226 pages across five sections**. Each section's landing page contains the full part list and prerequisite map; the file `curriculum.json` at the site root is the canonical machine-readable index.

### Section I: Linear Algebra to Algebraic Foundations (43 pages)
**URL**: [Mathematics/Linear_algebra/linear_algebra.html](https://math-cs-compass.com/Mathematics/Linear_algebra/linear_algebra.html)
**Tagline**: From Vector Spaces to Universal Algebraic Structures
Linear systems, vector spaces, eigenvalues, orthogonality, least squares, trace and norms, Kronecker and tensor products, the Schur complement and Woodbury identity, stochastic matrices, graph Laplacians and spectral methods; the Abstract Algebra block (finite groups, structural group theory, classification of finite abelian groups, rings, ideals and factor rings, polynomial rings, integral domains); extension fields, algebraic extensions, and finite fields (the arithmetic foundation for cryptography); the Lie group / Lie algebra series (matrix Lie groups, the matrix exponential, the Lie bracket, the Lie correspondence, complexification); a full **representation theory block** (irreducibility, constructions on representations, complete reducibility and Schur's lemma, the representations of sl(2;C), group vs. Lie algebra representations, Clebsch-Gordan theory, the Wigner-Eckart theorem, the Peter-Weyl theorem) — the continuous leg toward Geometric Deep Learning; and an algebraic entry into quantum computation, lattices, and Minkowski's theorem with the dual lattice.

### Section II: Calculus to Optimization & Analysis (99 pages)
**URL**: [Mathematics/Calculus/calculus.html](https://math-cs-compass.com/Mathematics/Calculus/calculus.html)
**Tagline**: The Mathematics of Change and Convergence
Derivatives (Jacobians, matrix calculus, scalar functions of a matrix); optimization (gradient descent, Newton's method, constrained optimization, duality); integration (Riemann, measure theory, Lebesgue); Fourier series and transforms; metric spaces (convergence, continuity, completeness, connectedness, compactness, equivalence) and topological spaces; the **Functional Analysis block** (Banach and Hilbert spaces, bounded linear operators, dual spaces and Riesz representation, weak topologies and Banach-Alaoglu, spectral theory of compact operators, RKHS, the Hahn-Banach theorem, the Baire category theorem and the three pillars, locally convex spaces, geometric Hahn-Banach and separation, the Krein-Milman theorem, the dual of C(X), Stone-Weierstrass); Lp space theory (construction, Hölder / Minkowski, completeness via Riesz-Fischer, convergence modes); classical PDEs (heat, wave, Laplace); and a large **smooth manifold track** — topological and smooth manifolds, charts and the Grassmannian, manifolds with boundary, homotopy and the fundamental group, partitions of unity, tangent vectors and the tangent bundle, the inverse and implicit function theorems, immersions/embeddings/submersions, submanifolds, Sard's theorem, the Whitney embedding theorem, Lie groups as smooth manifolds, group actions and equivariant maps, vector fields and the Lie bracket, integral curves and flows, Lie derivatives, vector bundles and fiber bundles, covectors and cotangent bundles, tensors and tensor fields, Riemannian metrics and the distance function, musical isomorphisms, alternating tensors and differential forms, the exterior derivative, orientations, the Riemannian volume form, integration of forms, and Stokes's theorem.

### Section III: Probability & Statistics (26 pages)
**URL**: [Mathematics/Probability/probability.html](https://math-cs-compass.com/Mathematics/Probability/probability.html)
**Tagline**: The Mathematics of Uncertainty and Inference
Classical probability and random variables; standard distributions (Gamma, Beta, Gaussian, Student's t); covariance, correlation, multivariate distributions; MLE, hypothesis testing, linear regression; entropy and information theory; convergence and limit theorems; Bayesian inference and the exponential family; the Fisher information matrix and information geometry foundations; Bayesian decision theory; Markov chains, Monte Carlo, importance sampling; Gaussian processes; the **measure-theoretic deepening track** — measure-theoretic probability, product measures and limit theorems, signed measures and the Radon-Nikodym theorem, conditional expectation as a Radon-Nikodym derivative — culminating in variational inference, and providing the foundations behind KL-regularized policies in RLHF, score-based diffusion models, and Bayesian neural networks.

### Section IV: Discrete Mathematics & Algorithms (40 pages)
**URL**: [Mathematics/Discrete/discrete_math.html](https://math-cs-compass.com/Mathematics/Discrete/discrete_math.html)
**Tagline**: The Mathematics of Logic and Finite Structures
Graph theory (Eulerian / Hamiltonian, network flow, trees, planar graphs); combinatorics; Boolean logic; the theory of computation (finite automata, context-free languages, Turing machines, time complexity, class NP); the **discrete topology arc** — incidence structures and cycle / cut spaces, simplicial complexes, an introduction to homology; **formal methods** — machine-verified proof and Lean in practice; a **category theory block** — categories, functors and natural transformations, adjunctions and universal properties, units and counits, the category of Sets, representable functors, the Yoneda lemma and its consequences, universal constructions (products, equalizers, pullbacks), limits and colimits of diagrams, and functors and limits — the foundation of the Categorical Deep Learning viewpoint; and a full **cryptography track** — introduction to cryptography, defining security, public-key cryptography, hardness assumptions, Diffie-Hellman and RSA, digital signatures, Shor's algorithm, and the post-quantum lattice sequence (lattice problems, Short Integer Solution, Learning With Errors, ring lattices and Module-LWE, zero-knowledge proofs), inheriting Section I's finite-field and lattice arithmetic.

### Section V: Machine Learning (18 pages)
**URL**: [Mathematics/Machine_learning/ml.html](https://math-cs-compass.com/Mathematics/Machine_learning/ml.html)
**Tagline**: The Mathematics of Synthesis and Intelligence
Foundations of machine learning; regularized regression and classification; neural networks and automatic differentiation; support vector machines and kernel methods; PCA, autoencoders, and clustering; deep neural networks (CNNs, ResNets, Self-Attention, Transformers); reinforcement learning; natural gradient descent on statistical manifolds; variational autoencoders; an introduction to **Geometric Deep Learning**; **diffusion models** and **flow matching**; **symmetry and representation theory in ML** (equivariant networks, the continuous GDL leg); **simplicial neural networks** (topological deep learning, the discrete GDL leg); and "Learning With Errors, Read Twice," which re-reads the lattice problem of Section IV as noisy linear regression on one hand and as a noise-budgeted ring homomorphism on the other. Topics here act as application viewpoints: Natural Gradient Descent previews Riemannian geometry before manifolds are formally introduced, Variational Autoencoders preview variational inference, and the GDL pages close the loop back to Section I's representation theory and Section II's manifold series.

## How to Navigate

- For **machine-readable curriculum data** (full part list, prerequisites, descriptions, anchor IDs): see [curriculum.json](https://math-cs-compass.com/curriculum.json) at the site root.
- For **interactive navigation**: the [Hexagonal Knowledge Map](https://math-cs-compass.com/map/compass.html) visualizes all topics and prerequisite paths.
- For **cross-references inside pages**: theorems and definitions carry stable IDs (`T-foo_bar`, `D-foo_bar`) consumed by the on-page hover-preview system; the registry is at `data/previews.json`.
- For **the site-wide bibliography**: see `data/references.json`.

## Technical Details

- **Platform**: Jekyll static site hosted on GitHub Pages.
- **Rendering**: MathJax for LaTeX, Mermaid for diagrams.
- **Interactive**: JavaScript / Python demos via Pyodide.
- **SEO**: Schema.org Course and LearningResource markup on each section page.

## Contact & License

- **Feedback**: Contact form available on the website.
- **License**: Educational content for free public access. Code samples follow standard open-source conventions (see GitHub).
